verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which when the Apostles, Barnabas and Paul, heard of, they rent their clothes, and ran in among the people, crying out and saying, Sirs, why do ye these things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We also are men of like passions with you, and preach unto you that ye should turn from these vanities unto the living God, which made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and all things that are therein.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the vehemence with which all this is done by the Apostles: “rent their clothes, ran in, cried out,” all from strong affection of the soul, revolted by the things that were done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was a grief, indeed a grief inconsolable, that they should needs be thought gods, and introduce idolatry, the very thing which they came to destroy!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This also was a contrivance of the devil—but he did not prevail.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what say they?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We also are men of like passions with you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the very outset they overthrew the evil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They said not simply, “Men,” but “As ye.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, that they may not seem to honor the gods, hear what they add: “Preaching unto you, that ye should turn from these vanities unto the living God, Who made heaven, the sea, and all things that are therein.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how they nowhere mention things invisible. (b) For they had learnt that one should study not so much to say somewhat worthy of God, as to say what is profitable for the hearers. (a) What then? if He be Maker of all things, why does He not also attend to these things by His Providence?—“Who in times past suffered all nations to walk in their own ways” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16)—but wherefore He suffered them, this he does not say, for at present he keeps to the matter of immediate importance, nowhere bringing in the name of Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, he does not wish to swell the accusation against them, but rather that they themselves should refer all to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Nevertheless, He left not Himself without witness, in that He did good, giving you rain from heaven, and fruitful seasons, filling your hearts with food and gladness.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17.) (c) See how covertly he puts the accusation “in that He did good,” etc. And yet if God did this, He could not have “let them alone;” on the contrary, they ought to be punished, for that, enjoying so great benefits, they had not acknowledged Him, not even as their feeder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “From heaven,” he says, “giving you rain.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also David said, “From the fruit of their corn and wine and oil were they made to abound” (Ps. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7), and in many places speaking of Creation, he brings forward these benefits: and Jeremiah mentions first Creation, then Providence (shown) by the rains, so that the Apostle here discourses as taught from those Scriptures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Filling,” he says, “with food and gladness.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With large liberality (φιλοτιμίας) the food is given, not merely for a frugal sufficiency, nor stinted by the need.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And saying these things, they scarcely stopped the multitudes” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 18)—indeed by this very thing they gained most admiration—“from sacrificing to them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe that this was the point with them to put an end to that madness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But there came,” it says, “certain Jews from Antioch and Iconium” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19).—Indeed children of the devil, that not in their own cities only, but also beyond them, they did these things, and as much made it their study to make an end of the preaching, as the Apostles were in earnest to establish it!—“and having persuaded the multitude and stoned Paul, they dragged him out of the city.” (e) So then, the Gentiles regarded them as gods, but these “dragged” him, “out of the city, supposing he had been dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having persuaded the multitude”—for it is not likely that all thus reverenced them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the very city in which they received this reverence, in the same were they thus terribly ill treated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this also profited the beholders.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Lest any man,” he says, “should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth aught from me.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20.)—“Howbeit as the disciples stood round about him, he rose up and came into the city.” (d) Here is fulfilled that saying, “My grace is sufficient for thee, for My strength is made perfect in weakness.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Cor. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Greater this than the raising of the lame man! (f) “Came into the city.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the zeal, do you mark how fervent he is, how set on fire!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He came into the city itself again: for proof that if on any occasion he did retire, it was because he had sown the word, and because it was not right to inflame their wrath. (h) Then they went over all the cities in which they had been in danger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And on the morrow,” it says, “he went forth with Barnabas to Derbe.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they had preached the Gospel to that city, and had taught many, they returned again to Lystra, and to Iconium, and Antioch, confirming the souls of the disciples, and exhorting them to continue in the faith, and that we must through much tribulation enter into the kingdom of God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This they said, this they showed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it is purposely so done, not only by the Apostles, but by the disciples also, that they may learn from the very outset both the might of the preaching, and that they must themselves also suffer such things, that they may stand nobly, not idly gaping for the miracles, but much more (ready) for the trials.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore also the Apostle himself said, “Having the same conflict which ye saw in me and heard.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Persecutions succeeded to persecutions: wars, fightings, stonings. (g) These things, not less than the miracles, both made them more illustrious, and prepared for them a greater rejoicing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Scripture nowhere says that they returned rejoicing because they had done miracles, but (it does say that they rejoiced), that “they were counted worthy for that Name to suffer shame.” (ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this they were taught of Christ, saying, “Rejoice not that the devils obey you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the joy indeed and without alloy is this, to suffer aught for Christ’s sake. (i) “And that through much tribulation:” what sort of cheering (προτροπή) is this? how did they persuade them, by telling them at the outset of tribulations?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then also another consolation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they had appointed for them elders in every Church, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they believed.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark Paul’s ardor?—Then other consolation: “Commended them,” it says, “to the Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And after they had passed throughout Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they had preached the word in Perga, they went down into Attalia (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24, 25): (l) and thence sailed to Antioch, from whence they had been recommended to the grace of God for the work which they fulfilled.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do they come back to Antioch?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To report what had taken place yonder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, there is a great purpose of Providence concerned: for it was needful that they should thenceforth preach with boldness to the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They come therefore, reporting these things, that they may be able to know them: and it is providentially ordered, that just then came those who forbade to keep company with the Gentiles in order that from Jerusalem they might obtain great encouragement, and so go their ways with boldness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, it shows that in their temper there was nothing of self-will: for they come, at the same time showing their boldness, in that without the authority of those (at Jerusalem) they had preached to the Gentiles, and their obedience, in that they refer the matter to them: for they were not made arrogant, as (ἀπενοήθησαν) having achieved so great successes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Whence,” it says, “they had been recommended to the grace of God for the work which they had fulfilled.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet moreover the Spirit had said, “Separate Me Barnabas and Saul for the work whereunto I have called them.” (ch. xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they were come, and had gathered the Church together, they rehearsed all that God had done with them, and how He had opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And there they abode long time with the disciples.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the city being great had need of teachers.—But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which when the Apostles,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: First by the sight they checked them, by rending their garments.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This did Joshua the son of Nun upon the occasion of the defeat of the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then think not that this action was unworthy of them: for such was the eagerness, they would not otherwise have restrained it would not otherwise have quenched the conflagration (πύραν).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore when need is to do something that is fit to be done, let us not decline it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if even after all this they hardly persuaded them, if they had not acted thus, what might have been the consequence?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if they had not done thus, they would have been thought to make a show of humility (ταπεινοθρονεἵν), and to be all the more desirous of the honor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe their language, how in rebuking it is moderated, alike full of wonder and of rebuke.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This above all it was that hindered them, the saying, “Preaching unto you to turn from these vanities unto God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are men indeed, they say, but greater than these: for these are dead things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how they not only subvert (the false), but teach (the true), saying nothing about things invisible—“Who made,” say they, “heaven and earth, and the sea, and all things that are therein.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who in times past,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He names as witnesses even the years (in their courses).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And there came thither certain Jews,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O that Jewish madness!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Among a people that had so honored the Apostles, they had the hardihood to come, and to stone Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they dragged him out of the city,” being afraid of those (others),—“Supposing he had been dead.” (k) “Howbeit,” etc. “and came into the city.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that the spirits of the disciples might not be downcast because they who were accounted gods suffered such treatment, they came in unto them and discoursed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then on the morrow,” etc. And observe, first he goes forth to Derbe, and then comes back to Lystra and Iconium and Antioch, (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21) giving way to them while their passions are roused, but when they have ceased, then attacking them again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark, that it was not by (supernatural) grace that they managed all that they did, but by their own diligence?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Confirming,” it says “the souls of the disciples:” ἐπιστηρίζοντες, “further establishing;” so that they were established, but they added more thereto.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And that we must,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22): they foretold (this), that they might not be offended.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they had appointed for them,” etc. Again the ordinations accompanied with fastings: and again fasting, that purifying of our souls. (m) “And having prayed,” it says, “with fastings, they commended them unto the Lord” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23): they taught them to fast also in their trials. (o) Why did they not make elders in Cyprus nor in Samaria?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because the latter was near to Jerusalem, the former to Antioch, and the word was strong there; whereas in those parts they needed much consolation, especially they of the Gentiles, who behooved to have much instruction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they were come,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They came, teaching them that with good reason had they been ordained by the Spirit. (n) They said not what they themselves, but “what God had done with them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems to me, that they mean their trials.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not for nothing that they come here, nor to rest, but providentially guided by the Spirit, to the end that the preaching to the Gentiles might be firmly established. (p) And mark Paul’s ardor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not ask whether it be right to speak to Gentiles, but he straightway speaks: therefore it is that he says, “I did not refer myself to flesh and blood.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is indeed a great thing, a great, a generous soul (like this)!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many have since believed, and none of them all has shone like him!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What we want is earnestness, exceeding ardor, a soul ready to encounter death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Else is it not possible to attain unto the Kingdom, not being crucified.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not deceive ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if in war it is impossible to come off safe while living daintily, and trafficking, and huckstering and idling, much more in this war.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or think ye not that it is a war worse than all others?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For we wrestle not,” he says, “against flesh and blood.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since even while taking our meals and walking, and bathing, the enemy is present with us, and knows no time of truce, except that of sleep only: nay, often even then he carries on the war, injecting into us unclean thoughts, and making us lewd by means of dreams.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We watch not, we do not rouse ourselves up, do not look to the multitude of the forces opposed to us, do not reflect, that this very thing constitutes the greatest misfortune—that though surrounded by so great wars, we live daintily as in time of peace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Believe me, worse than Paul suffered may have to be suffered now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those enemies wounded him with stones: there is a wounding with words, even worse than stones.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then must we do?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same that he did: he did not hate those who cast stones at him, but after they had dragged him out, he entered again into their city, to be a benefactor to those who had done him such wrongs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou also endurest him who harshly insults thee, and has done thee wrongs, then hast thou too been stoned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say not, “I have done him no injury.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what injury had Paul done, that he should be stoned?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was announcing a Kingdom, he was bringing men away from error, and bringing them to God: benefits these, worthy of crowns, worthy of proclamation by voice of herald, worthy of a thousand good things—not of stones.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet (far from resenting) he did just the contrary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is the splendid victory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they dragged him,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19) it says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These too they often drag: but be not thou angry; on the contrary, preach thou the word with gentleness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hath one insulted thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hold thy peace, and bless if thou canst, and thou also hast preached the word, hast given a lesson of gentleness, a lesson of meekness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I know that many do not so smart under wounds, as they do under the blow which is inflicted by words: as indeed the one wound the body receives the other the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us not smart, or rather feeling the smart let us endure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see the pugilists, how, with their heads sorely battered, they bite their teeth into their lips, and so bear their smarts kindly?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No need to grind the teeth, no need to bite (the lips).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Remember thy Master, and by the remembrance thou hast at once applied the remedy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Remember Paul: reflect that thou, the beaten hast conquered, and he the beater, is defeated; and by this hast thou cured the whole.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is the turning of the scale a moment and thou hast achieved the whole: be not hurried away, do not even move, thou hast extinguished the whole (fire).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great eloquence of persuasion there is in suffering aught for Christ: thou preachest not the word of faith, but thou preachest the word of patience (φιλοσοφίας).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, you will say, the more he sees my gentleness, the more he sets upon me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it for this then that thou art pained, that he increases thy rewards the more?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But this is the way,” you say, “to make him unbearable.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is mere pretext of thine own littleness of mind: on the contrary, the other is the way to make him unbearable, namely, that thou avenge thyself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If God had known, that through forbearance of revenge, the unjust became unbearable, He would not have done this Himself: on the contrary, He would have said, Avenge thyself: but He knew, that other than this is the more likely way to do good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make not thou a law contrary to God: do as He bids thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou art not kinder than He that made us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He hath said, “Bear to be wronged:” thou sayest, “I requite wrong for wrong, that he may not become unbearable.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hast thou then more care for him than God has?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such talk is mere passion and ill temper, arrogance and setting up laws against God’s laws.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For even if the man were hurt (by our forbearance), would it not be our duty to obey?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When God orders anything, let us not make a contrary law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A submissive answer,” we read, turneth away wrath” (Prov. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1): not an answer of opposition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If it profits thee, it profits him also: but if it hurts thee who art to set him right, how much more will it hurt him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Physician, heal thyself.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hath one spoken ill of thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Commend him thou.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hath he reviled thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Praise him thou.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hath he plotted against thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do him a kindness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Requite him with the contrary things, if at least thou at all carest for his salvation and wish not thou to revenge thine own suffering.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet, you will say, though he has often met with long-suffering from me he has become worse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is not thine affair, but his.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wilt thou learn what wrongs God suffered?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They threw down His altars, and slew His prophets (1 Kings xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10), yet He endured it all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Could He not have launched a thunderbolt from above?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, when He had sent His prophets, and they killed them, then He sent His Son (Matt. xxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 37), when they wrought greater impieties, then He sent them greater benefits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And thou too, if thou seest one exasperated, then yield the more: since this madness has greater need of soothing (παραμθίας).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The more grievous his abuse of thee, the more meekness does he need from thee: and even as a gale when it blows strong, then it requires yielding to, so also he who is in a passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When the wild beast is most savage, then we all flee: so also should we flee from him that is angry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think not that this is an honor to him: for is it an honor we show to the wild beast, and to madmen, when we turn aside out of their way?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means it is a dishonor and a scorn: or rather not dishonor and scorn, but compassion and humanity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou not how the sailors, when the wind blows violently, take down their sails, that the vessel may not sink? how, when the horses have run away with the driver, he only leads them into the (open) plain, and does not pull against them that he may not voluntarily exhaust his strength?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This do thou also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wrath is afire, it is a quick flame needing fuel: do not supply food to the fire, and thou hast soon extinguished the evil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Anger has no power of itself; there must be another to feed it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For thee there is no excuse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is possessed with madness, and knows not what he does; but when thou, seeing what he is, fallest into the same evils, and art not brought to thy right senses by the sight, what excuse can there be for thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If coming to a feast thou see at the very outset of the feast some one drunken and acting unseemly, would not he, who after seeing him makes himself drunk, be much more inexcusable?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just so it is here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do we think it any excuse to say, I was not the first to begin?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is against us, that even the sight of the other in that condition did not bring us to our right senses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is just as if one should say, “I did not murder him first.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this very thing makes thee deserving of punishment, that even upon the warning of such a spectacle thou didst not restrain thyself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou shouldest see the drunken man in the act of vomiting, retching, bursting, his eyes strained, filling the table with his filthiness, everybody hurrying out of his way, and then shouldest fall into the same state thyself, wouldest thou not be more hateful?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like him is he that is in a passion: more than he who vomits, he has his veins distended, his eyes inflamed, his bowels racked; he vomits forth words far more filthy than that food; all crude what he utters, nothing duly digested, for his passion will not let it be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as in that case excess of fumes (χυμων), making an uproar in the stomach, often rejects all its contents; so here, excess of heat, making a tumult in the soul suffers him not to conceal what it were right to leave unsaid, but things fit and unfit to be spoken, he says all alike, not putting the hearers but himself to shame.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As then we get out of the way of those that vomit, so let us from those who are angry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us cast dust upon their vomit: By doing what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By holding our peace: let us call the dogs to eat up the vomit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I know that ye are disgusted at hearing this: but I wish you to feel this same disgust when ye see these things take place, and not to be pleased at the thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The abusive man is filthier than the dog that returneth to its own vomit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if indeed having vomited once he were done with it, he would not be like that dog: but if he vomits the same things again, it is plain that he does so from having eaten the same again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then is more abominable than such an one?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What filthier than that mouth which chews such food?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet this is a work of nature, but the other not or rather both the one and the other are contrary to nature.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since it is not according to nature to be causelessly abusive, but against nature: he speaks nothing then like a man, but part as beast, part as madman.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As then the disease of the body is contrary to nature, so also is this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And to show that it is contrary to nature, if he shall continue in it, he will perish by little and little: but if he continue in that which is natural, he will not perish.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I had rather sit at table with a man who eats dirt, than with one who speaks such words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See ye not the swine devouring dung?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So also do these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what is more stinking than the words which abusive men utter?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is their study to speak nothing wholesome, nothing pure, but whatever is base, whatever is unseemly, that they study both to do and say: and what is worse, they think to disgrace others, while they in fact are disgracing themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that it is themselves they disgrace is plain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, leaving out of the question those who speak lies (in their railings), say it be some notorious harlot, or even from the stage some other (abandoned creature), and let that person be having a fight with some other person: then let the latter cast this up to the former (what she or he is), and the former retort upon the latter the same reproach: which of them is most damaged by the words?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the former is but called what in fact he or she is, which is not the case with the other: so that the first gets nothing more in the way of shame (than there was before), while to the other there accrues a great accession of disgrace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But again, let there be some hidden actions (mod. text εἰργασμένα “which have been done”), and let only the person abusing know of them: then, holding his peace until now, let him openly parade (ἐκπομπευέτω) the reproach: even so, he himself is more disgraced than the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How? by making himself the herald of the wickedness, so getting for himself either the imputation of not being privy to any such thing, or the character of one not fit to be trusted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And you shall see all men forthwith accuse him: “If indeed he had been privy to a murder being done, he ought to have revealed it all:” and so they regard him with aversion as not human even, they hate him, they say he is a wild beast, fierce and cruel: while the other they pardon much rather than him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For we do not so much hate those that have wounds, as those that compel one to uncover and show them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus that man has not only disgraced the other, but himself as well and his hearers, and the common nature of men: he has wounded the hearer, done no good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this reason Paul says: “If there be any word that is good for edifying, that it may minister grace unto the hearers.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us get a tongue speaking only good things, that we may be lovely and amiable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But indeed, everything is come to that pitch of wickedness, that many boast of the very things, for which they should hide their faces.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the threats of the many are of this kind: “thou canst not bear my tongue,” say they.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Words, these, worthy only of a woman, of an abandoned drunken old hag, one of those that are dragged (to punishment) in the forum, a procuress.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing more shameful than these words, nothing more unmanly, more womanlike, than to have your strength in the tongue, and to think great things of yourself because you can rail, just like the fellows in processions, like the buffoons, parasites, and flatterers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Swine they are rather than men, who pride themselves upon this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whereas you should (sooner) have buried yourself, and if another gave you this character, should recoil from the charge as odious and unmanly, instead of that you have made yourself the herald of (your own) disgrace (ὓβρεων).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But you will not be able to hurt him you speak ill of.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore I beseech you, considering how the wickedness is come to such a height, that many boast of it, let us return to our senses, let us recover those who are thus mad, let us take away these councils out of the city, let us make our tongue gracious, let us rid it of all evil speaking, that being clean from sins, we may be able to draw down upon us the good-will from above, and to have mercy vouchsafed unto us from God, through the grace and compassion of His only-begotten Son, with Whom to the Father, together with the Holy Spirit, be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
